stakeholders.name: CAA Case Officer (REQ001)
stakeholders.specific_need: All mandatory document types and sections present on first Readiness Check to avoid rework cycles and case rejection

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: ConOps, SORA, Safety Case sections complete before formal review to assess hazard coverage

stakeholders.name: CAA SORA Assessor (REQ002)
stakeholders.specific_need: SORA evidence pack substantive and swarm-aware before setpoint assignment

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Clear definition of what constitutes completeness to avoid rejection and rework cost; specific acceptance criteria upfront
stakeholders.tolerable_override: Lower bar on first submission (e.g., 80% completeness with remediation plan acceptable)

stakeholders.name: CAA Programme Manager (REQ016)
stakeholders.specific_need: Completeness enables phased gates and predictable trial progression schedule

conflicts: Applicant wants flexibility on first submission (incomplete pack with remediation path acceptable); Case Officer wants 100% completeness on submission (no rework cycles). Conflict resolved by Case Officer authority and DeFOSPAM clarification that 'complete' means all mandatory types present and substantive content in each.

DeFOSPAM Dorothy-8 identified that 'complete' has two meanings (REQ001: application completeness vs REQ012: data logging completeness). Stakeholder conflict: Case Officer interprets completeness as pack intake; Flight Safety Investigator interprets it as forensic evidence retention. Resolved by distinct scale parameters.
reasoning: The Case Officer is the gate-keeper for case opening and has highest power and motivation. Safety-Case Reviewer depends on Case Officer delivering a substantially complete pack. Applicant motivation is cost-driven (rework avoidance) but lacks regulatory power. Clear definition of completeness (all mandatory types + substantive sections) serves both regulator and applicant.

stakeholders.name: CAA SORA Assessor (REQ002)
stakeholders.specific_need: Explicit swarm failure modes (loss-of-separation, loss-of-coordination, emergent failures) quantified in risk evidence so SAIL and OSO setpoints are defensible

stakeholders.name: CAA Autonomy Reviewer (REQ008)
stakeholders.specific_need: Swarm interdependency and emergent behavior evidence to assure collective decision-making safety

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: Swarm hazards and boundaries reflected in ConOps and ODD (worst-credible swarm behaviors explicitly modeled)

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Safety Manager
stakeholders.specific_need: Clear definition of 'swarm-aware' and minimum evidence items required (3 per aircraft) to avoid open-ended regulatory demands; cost-effective testing approach
stakeholders.target_override: Lower minimum count (e.g., 2 items per aircraft) if coordination mechanism and loss-of-separation evidence present

stakeholders.name: Applicant Autonomy Engineer
stakeholders.specific_need: Quantified bounds on emergent behaviors and coordination latency to guide design and testing

conflicts: SORA Assessor and Autonomy Reviewer want maximal swarm-specific evidence (all failure cascades enumerated, all emergent behaviors bounded); Applicant prefers minimal viable evidence to reduce engineering and testing cost. Conflict resolved by scale requiring >= 3 items per aircraft covering interdependency, emergent behavior, and coordination—a middle ground forcing explicit attention to swarm safety without unrealistic completeness.

Definition of 'swarm-aware' differs: regulator = evidence addressing multi-aircraft failure modes; applicant = evidence that coordination mechanism is reliable. Resolved by Alexa's scale specifying three sub-scales (interdependency, emergent behavior, coordination mechanism) capturing both intent.
reasoning: SORA Assessor drives risk classification and mandatory operating limits. Without clear swarm-aware evidence, SAIL/OSO setpoints are indefensible and applicant faces rejection. Autonomy Reviewer shares SORA Assessor's concern (collective safety). Applicant must provide evidence but negotiates scope. Primary stakeholder is SORA Assessor because SORA findings are the regulatory anchor for the trial.

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: Explicit definition of nominal and worst-credible behaviors for each flight phase (takeoff, transit, loiter, landing, etc.) so that two independent reviewers score clarity identically and hazard scope is unambiguous

stakeholders.name: CAA Flight-Ops Inspector (REQ011)
stakeholders.specific_need: Clear ConOps boundaries inform contingency planning: what behaviors trigger termination vs crew takeover vs autonomous recovery

stakeholders.name: CAA Airspace Regulator (REQ004, REQ005)
stakeholders.specific_need: ConOps worst-credible boundaries (max altitude, drift distance, airspace boundaries) to define Temporary Danger Area and geofence setpoints

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Clear definition of 'worst-credible' boundary (is it mean + 3-sigma? absolute worst-case physics? real test data?) to bound design and testing scope

stakeholders.name: Flight Test Engineer
stakeholders.specific_need: Quantified nominal and worst-credible parameters to design test cases and acceptance criteria

stakeholders.name: MAA Range Safety Officer
stakeholders.specific_need: ConOps worst-credible boundary definition to set range impact zones and test clearances (if trial is on MOD range)

conflicts: Safety-Case Reviewer demands inter-rater clarity (kappa >= 0.8); regulator reviewers may disagree on what constitutes 'worst-credible' (statistical vs absolute). Applicant prefers bounded definition (e.g., mean + 3-sigma) to limit testing; Safety Reviewer may demand absolute worst-case to close all scenarios. Conflict resolved by requiring explicit definition upfront + test evidence of reviewer agreement.

Airspace Regulator wants conservative (large) worst-credible boundaries to protect other airspace users and set wide TDA/geofence margins; Applicant wants tight boundaries to reduce trial footprint and enable higher operational ceiling. Conflict resolved by CAA Airspace Regulator power and safety priority; applicant must justify tight boundaries with strong evidence.
reasoning: Safety-Case Reviewer is the central gatekeeper for ConOps/ODD approval. Clear ConOps boundaries enable all downstream decisions (airspace segregation, geofence setpoints, contingency triggers, termination rules). Flight-Ops Inspector and Airspace Regulator depend on Safety Reviewer's ConOps approval. Applicant must invest in clarity upfront but benefits from unambiguous regulatory requirements.

stakeholders.name: CAA Airspace Regulator (REQ005)
stakeholders.specific_need: Automatic geofence containment with 99.9% success across Monte Carlo failure scenarios to ensure fly-away risk is eliminated and TDA can be tightly bounded

stakeholders.name: Third-Party Air Users (civil aviation)
stakeholders.specific_need: Hard containment guarantee so that swarm cannot drift into their airspace and create collision risk. Zero-tolerance for boundary exceedance.
stakeholders.target_override: 100% containment (no exceptions); single breach is unacceptable

stakeholders.name: Public Below (non-segregated operations / ground risk)
stakeholders.specific_need: Geofence prevents uncontrolled aircraft drift into populated areas. Protection from fly-away risk.

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Flexibility in geofence boundaries and design approach (hard constraint vs soft recovery vs crew intervention) to manage design and certification cost. Wants proportional risk-based requirements.
stakeholders.tolerable_override: 98% or 99% success if crew intervention is available on-site and response time is < 5 seconds
stakeholders.target_override: 95% success if multi-redundant termination is demonstrated

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: Geofence failure modes and worst-credible recovery time specified in ConOps to assess hazard mitigation completeness

stakeholders.name: MAA Range Safety Officer
stakeholders.specific_need: If trial on MOD range: geofence ensures aircraft cannot exit range boundary. Hard containment required.

conflicts: CRITICAL CONFLICT: Airspace Regulator demands 99.9% success (hard containment); Applicant wants proportional requirement based on crew intervention availability and response time (98-99% acceptable if crew is present). Third-Party Air Users and Public want zero breaches. Conflict resolution: CAA Airspace Regulator power is absolute; geofence is a hard safety requirement. Applicant cannot negotiate target downward; must invest in redundancy and Monte Carlo validation.

Design approach conflict: Applicant may propose soft geofence (warning boundary) with crew takeover; Airspace Regulator requires hard geofence (automatic motor cutoff or autonomous containment maneuver). Conflict resolved by regulatory safety priority: hard containment is non-negotiable.

Test scope conflict: Applicant wants limited failure mode coverage (single failures); CAA demands comprehensive Monte Carlo including dual failures, GPS denial, sensor faults, and software faults. Conflict resolved by scale requiring dual failure coverage and environmental ODD bounds.
reasoning: Airspace Regulator has absolute power to approve or reject geofence design and is accountable to Third-Party Air Users and Public. Without 99.9% containment evidence, TDA cannot be safely approved. Third-Party Air Users and Public depend on Airspace Regulator enforcement. Applicant must meet this hard requirement; no negotiation on target. DeFOSPAM Dorothy-3 and Observation-2 flagged that containment breach action is undefined; Alexa's scale operationalizes the 99.9% success criterion.

stakeholders.name: CAA Spectrum Specialist (REQ006)
stakeholders.specific_need: C2 link loss survival times quantified (>= 30 seconds autonomous, >= 120 seconds with crew takeover) to assess non-interference with other users and spectrum efficiency

stakeholders.name: CAA Flight-Ops Inspector (REQ011)
stakeholders.specific_need: Link loss response (failover behavior, crew takeover latency, termination decision) defined to ensure safe contingency execution

stakeholders.name: CAA DAA Assessor (REQ007)
stakeholders.specific_need: If C2 loss triggers autonomous DA logic, inter-vehicle coordination must remain safe. Link loss affects both C2 and inter-vehicle data; evidence required.

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Autonomous survival time threshold and crew takeover latency defined upfront to design C2 redundancy, failover logic, and user interface. Wants achievable targets (e.g., 30 sec autonomous is feasible with modern autopilots)
stakeholders.target_override: Lower survival times (e.g., 15 seconds autonomous, 60 seconds with crew takeover) if ground-based recovery is available

stakeholders.name: Flight Test Engineer
stakeholders.specific_need: Defined test scenarios for C2 loss (complete loss, partial degradation, inter-vehicle link loss, combined GPS+C2 loss) to structure flight test and acceptance criteria

stakeholders.name: Applicant Autonomy Engineer
stakeholders.specific_need: Inter-vehicle link loss behavior defined: does swarm split? Return to base individually? Maintain formation? Bounds on behavior during link loss inform algorithm design.

conflicts: Spectrum Specialist demands high survival times (>= 30 sec autonomous) to prove non-interference; Applicant may want lower thresholds (e.g., 15 sec) as easier to achieve. Conflict resolved by Spectrum Specialist regulatory authority and realistic automation capability (modern autopilots can sustain 30-60 seconds safely).

Flight-Ops Inspector wants crew takeover available as primary failover (>= 120 sec window); Applicant may propose autonomous termination instead (cost lower, crew not needed). Conflict: human factors and meaningful control requirements favor crew takeover; Alexa's scale requires both paths quantified but defaults to crew takeover + autonomous survival.

Inter-vehicle link loss scenario: Applicant prefers simple behavior (split to base individually); Regulators want swarm to maintain formation if possible (preserves safety case). Conflict resolved by requiring both sub-cases tested and behavior pre-defined in ConOps.
reasoning: Spectrum Specialist has regulatory authority over C2 link performance and must defend non-interference to other spectrum users (civil aviation, emergency services). Flight-Ops Inspector shares responsibility (contingency management). Applicant must design and test to meet 30-second survival minimum. Link loss is a critical failure mode (loss-of-separation risk) and Spectrum Specialist controls the approval threshold.

stakeholders.name: CAA DAA Assessor (REQ007)
stakeholders.specific_need: Detect-and-avoid resolution is predictable and consistent (>= 95% decision consistency, >= 500ft horizontal recovery) so that encounter outcomes are defensible and DO-365 / F3442 compliance is demonstrable

stakeholders.name: CAA Airspace Regulator (REQ004, REQ005)
stakeholders.specific_need: DAA system prevents loss-of-separation with non-cooperative aircraft in uncontrolled airspace. Resolution must not trigger secondary conflicts or endanger third parties.

stakeholders.name: Third-Party Air Users (civil aviation, commercial, general aviation)
stakeholders.specific_need: Swarm DAA response is predictable and non-aggressive so that encounter is resolved safely without forcing third-party maneuvers or creating secondary conflict

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Clear definition of 'predictable' and 'consistent' with quantified success thresholds (>= 95% decision consistency, >= 100 encounter test scenarios) to bound development and testing scope
stakeholders.target_override: Lower consistency threshold (e.g., 90%) if encounter type distribution is representative and coverage includes corner cases

stakeholders.name: Applicant Autonomy Engineer
stakeholders.specific_need: Defined encounter types (cooperative ADS-B, non-cooperative radar, pop-up) and decision rules to guide DAA algorithm design and testing. Reproducible test framework for consistency measurement.

stakeholders.name: CAA Flight-Ops Inspector (REQ011)
stakeholders.specific_need: DAA resolution behavior known and practiced in crew training so that crew can monitor and override if needed (meaningful human control)

conflicts: DAA Assessor wants maximum encounter coverage (all geometry types, all relative speeds, all weather conditions) to prove robustness; Applicant wants bounded test scenarios (representative sample of 100 encounters) to limit cost. Conflict resolved by DO-365 / F3442 standard applicability; Applicant must meet industry-standard encounter distribution.

Third-Party Air Users expect zero unpredictable resolutions (100% consistency); DAA Assessor sets realistic threshold (>= 95%) to acknowledge edge cases. Conflict resolved by regulatory authority: >= 95% consistency is the gate; residual 5% documented and acceptable.

Swarm coordination on encounter: Applicant may prefer single-aircraft DA response (each aircraft independently avoids); Airspace Regulator and DAA Assessor want coordinated swarm response to maintain formation and avoid secondary conflicts. Conflict: scale requires both swarm coordination on encounter and decision consistency.
reasoning: DAA Assessor has regulatory authority over detect-and-avoid performance and must defend consistency and predictability to Third-Party Air Users and Airspace Regulator. DO-365 / F3442 standards are the regulatory anchor. Applicant must design and test to meet >= 95% consistency and zero secondary conflicts. Third-Party Air Users have power (can formally object to trial) and depend on DAA Assessor's approval.

stakeholders.name: CAA Autonomy Reviewer (REQ008)
stakeholders.specific_need: Emergent behaviors identified, bounded, and tested (>= 5 behaviors, quantified limits, >= 95% flight test hours in bounds) to assure collective swarm behavior is predictable and safe

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: Emergent behavior definitions and boundaries reflected in ConOps and ODD so that worst-credible collective behaviors are explicitly scoped in hazard analysis

stakeholders.name: CAA Flight-Ops Inspector (REQ011)
stakeholders.specific_need: Out-of-bounds emergent behavior triggers defined (detection mechanism + recovery action) to inform contingency planning and crew training

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Clear definition of 'emergent behavior' and minimum count (>= 5) to bound analysis scope; detection/recovery mechanisms must be feasible to implement and test
stakeholders.tolerable_override: Lower count (>= 3) if core behaviors (formation stability, coordinated maneuver, flocking) are thoroughly analyzed and tested

stakeholders.name: Applicant Autonomy Engineer
stakeholders.specific_need: Emergent behaviors quantified and detection criteria defined to guide swarm architecture design and flight testing. Wants proportional analysis (focus on safety-critical behaviors like loss-of-separation, emergent cohesion loss, unintended split)

conflicts: Autonomy Reviewer wants exhaustive emergent behavior inventory (formation geometry drift, coordination latency, flocking instability, split/rejoin behavior, multi-vehicle collision avoidance); Applicant wants focus on safety-critical behaviors only (formation loss-of-separation, coordinated maneuver stability). Conflict resolved by Alexa's scale requiring >= 5 distinct behaviors with quantified boundaries and test evidence—applicant must justify each behavior as either safety-critical or safety-relevant.

Detection mechanism conflict: Autonomy Reviewer wants automatic detection and autonomous recovery/abort (safety-critical); Applicant may prefer crew monitoring and manual intervention (lower automation cost). Conflict resolved by meaningful human control requirements (REQ010)—crew must be in the loop, but detection can be automatic.

Test coverage: Autonomy Reviewer wants >= 95% of flight test hours in bounds (stringent); Applicant prefers lower threshold if out-of-bounds occurrences are rare and handled safely. Conflict: scale requires >= 95% to assure boundedness; residual 5% must be documented and analyzed.
reasoning: Autonomy Reviewer has regulatory authority over swarm decision-making and emergent behavior assurance. Without bounded emergent behaviors, swarm is unpredictable and uninsurable. Safety-Case Reviewer depends on Autonomy Reviewer's conclusions. Applicant must invest in behavioral analysis and flight testing to identify, quantify, and bound emergent behaviors. DeFOSPAM Observation-6 flagged that emergent deviation outcomes are undefined; Alexa's scale operationalizes this by requiring pre-defined recovery mechanisms.

stakeholders.name: CAA AI/ML Assurance Specialist (REQ009)
stakeholders.specific_need: ML decision logic is verifiable by independent reviewers (decision trees = 100%, neural networks = LIME/SHAP with >= 80% coverage) so that safety assurance case can be closed and operational deployment is defensible

stakeholders.name: AAIB / Flight Safety Investigator (post-incident)
stakeholders.specific_need: ML model decisions captured and explainable from logged decision inputs and feature saliency so that incident root cause can be determined forensically
stakeholders.target_override: Maximal explainability (every decision path reconstructable); zero black-box models

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Autonomy Engineer
stakeholders.specific_need: Practical definition of 'forensically explainable' with model-type-specific approaches so that cost-prohibitive explainability requirements are avoided (e.g., decision trees acceptable, neural networks require SHAP, SVMs require feature importance)
stakeholders.target_override: Lower explainability threshold for non-safety-critical decisions (e.g., performance optimization) vs safety-critical decisions (full reconstruction required)

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: ML components assurable in safety case so that hazard mitigation claims can be verified. Explainability is one part of broader assurance case (training data provenance, validation performance, operational monitoring)

stakeholders.name: Defence Aviation Safety Board / MOD Safety Authority
stakeholders.specific_need: If trial uses classified data or MOD models, explainability must be demonstrable without revealing classified information. Security vs transparency trade-off.

conflicts: CRITICAL CONFLICT: AAIB wants maximal explainability (every decision path reconstructable, zero black-box); Applicant wants practical threshold (neural networks with SHAP >= 80% coverage acceptable). Conflict: AI/ML Assurance Specialist and AAIB have power (post-incident investigation authority); applicant must invest in explainability. Alexa's scale operationalizes pragmatic approach: decision-tree models = 100%, neural networks = saliency maps + LIME/SHAP with >= 80% coverage. This is middle ground but weighted toward explainability.

Cost vs assurance: Applicant prefers simpler models (SVMs, decision trees) to avoid explainability cost; Autonomy Engineer prefers neural networks (better performance on swarm coordination decisions). Conflict: explainability requirement incentivizes simpler models; applicant must choose between performance and cost.

Safety-critical vs performance: Applicant wants lower explainability for performance-optimization decisions (e.g., trajectory smoothing); AI/ML Specialist argues all swarm-coordination decisions are safety-critical. Conflict: scale defaults to safety-critical = full reconstruction, non-safety-critical = feature importance sufficient. Burden is on applicant to justify low-criticality classification.
reasoning: AI/ML Assurance Specialist has regulatory authority over ML assurance case and must defend explainability to AAIB and Safety-Case Reviewer. AAIB (post-incident) has power to demand maximum forensic detail. Applicant must design, train, and document ML models with explainability in mind from the start. DeFOSPAM Dorothy-4 flagged 'forensically explainable' as unrealistic for neural networks; Alexa's scale operationalizes pragmatic model-type-specific approaches. MLForensicExplainability is a primary conflict hotspot (cost vs assurance vs performance).

stakeholders.name: CAA Human Factors Inspector (REQ010)
stakeholders.specific_need: Crew situational awareness (>= 2Hz nominal, >= 10Hz off-nominal), authority preservation (100% on irreversible, >= 80% on reversible decisions), takeover latency (>= 10 seconds), and workload bounds (>= 3 concurrent alarms, <= 6 min/hour control) demonstrate meaningful human control throughout

stakeholders.name: CAA Autonomy Reviewer (REQ008)
stakeholders.specific_need: Autonomous decision boundaries and override capability defined so that crew can interrupt and regain control in off-nominal states (relates to emergent behavior recovery)

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Quantified meaningful control thresholds (awareness frequency, authority percentage, latency bounds) to design crew interface and training, avoiding open-ended 'meaningful' requirement
stakeholders.tolerable_override: Lower state update frequency (1Hz nominal) if crew interface displays aggregate swarm health in real-time
stakeholders.target_override: Higher automation (< 80% authority preservation) if autonomous safety margins are wide and crew training emphasizes monitoring vs intervention

stakeholders.name: Applicant Flight Test Crew (Pilot, Sensor Operator)
stakeholders.specific_need: Crew interface and control latency enable safe and usable monitoring and intervention during flight test. Not overly workload-intensive. Tangible feedback on system state.

stakeholders.name: CAA Flight-Ops Inspector (REQ011)
stakeholders.specific_need: Crew takeover and termination decision points clear in ConOps so that emergency procedures can be trained and practiced

stakeholders.name: RAISO / Range Safety Officer
stakeholders.specific_need: If on MOD range: crew control must enable range safety officer to interdict flight if needed. Meaningful control supports range safety integration.

conflicts: CRITICAL CONFLICT: Human Factors Inspector wants high control authority (100% on irreversible, >= 80% on reversible) and fast takeover (>= 10 sec); Applicant prefers high autonomy (>= 90% autonomous decisions) to reduce crew workload and cost. Conflict: meaningful human control is a regulatory principle (CAP 722 requirement); Applicant must accept >= 80% authority preservation on reversible decisions. Takeover latency (10 seconds) is achievable with modern autopilots and crew interfaces.

Situational awareness trade-off: Human Factors Inspector wants high update frequency (>= 2Hz nominal, >= 10Hz off-nominal) to enable meaningful control; Applicant wants lower frequency (1Hz) to reduce interface complexity and crew workload. Conflict: scale requires >= 2Hz nominal (industry standard for UAS situational awareness); off-nominal can be negotiated down to 5Hz if safety-critical alerts are instant.

Workload vs autonomy: Applicant wants high autonomy to reduce crew mental load (cost-driven); Human Factors Inspector worries that over-automation creates monitoring fatigue and complacency (safety concern). Conflict: scale balances both by limiting concurrent alarms (>= 3) and active control duty cycle (<= 6 min/hour) so crew remains engaged without burnout.
reasoning: Human Factors Inspector has regulatory authority over meaningful human control and crew fitness and must defend control authority to CAA, Parliament (EU Regulation 2019/945 compliance), and public. Applicant prefers automation (lower cost) but regulatory requirement forces human-in-the-loop design. DeFOSPAM Dorothy-2 flagged 'meaningful' as undefined; Alexa's scale operationalizes it via four measurable dimensions (awareness, authority, latency, workload). MeaningfulHumanControl is a top conflict hotspot (automation vs safety).

stakeholders.name: CAA Flight-Ops Inspector (REQ011)
stakeholders.specific_need: Independent flight termination effective in any off-nominal state (loss of autonomy, loss of C2, loss of geofence, engine failure, etc.) with >= 50 test scenarios and completion within defined time (e.g., <= 10 seconds) so that trial can be safely terminated

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: Termination capability and contingency plan documented in ConOps as final safety layer. Worst-credible scenarios must include termination failure and safe-fallback configuration.

stakeholders.name: CAA Flight Safety Investigator (REQ012)
stakeholders.specific_need: Post-termination actions (crew alert, ATSU notification, data preservation, recovery) defined so that incident investigation is not delayed by recovery operations

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Termination mechanism and failure modes defined (e.g., dual-redundant motor cutoff, parachute, controlled spiral descent) so that design scope is bounded and cost is predictable
stakeholders.target_override: Single-channel termination if monitoring/verification proves sufficient; dual-channel if failure history suggests failure rate >= 1e-4

stakeholders.name: Applicant Flight Test Engineer
stakeholders.specific_need: Termination test scenarios defined (loss-of-autonomy, loss-of-C2, loss-of-geofence, engine failure, collision threat, environmental extreme) to structure flight test plan

stakeholders.name: Third-Party Air Users
stakeholders.specific_need: Swarm can be reliably terminated and brought to safe configuration (no powered flight, minimal drift) if off-nominal state is detected so that collision risk is mitigated

conflicts: Single vs dual redundancy: Flight-Ops Inspector prefers dual-channel termination (fail-safe); Applicant wants single-channel (lower cost) if reliability is proven. Conflict: scale requires >= 50 test scenarios covering all failure modes; applicant must demonstrate high reliability (MTBF >= 1e6 hours) for single-channel to be acceptable.

Termination mechanism: Flight-Ops Inspector may prefer parachute or controlled descent (proven); Applicant prefers autonomous motor cutoff (lower cost, simpler). Conflict: mechanism must achieve safe configuration (motors off, drift ceased) within defined time; applicant chooses mechanism but must prove safety.

Post-termination actions: Flight-Ops Inspector wants comprehensive logging and ATSU notification (safety accountability); Applicant wants minimal overhead (cost-driven). Conflict: scale requires all post-termination actions (crew alert, data preservation, recovery notification) defined and tested; non-negotiable for regulatory approval.
reasoning: Flight-Ops Inspector has regulatory authority over flight termination and contingency planning and must defend termination reliability to Safety-Case Reviewer and Third-Party Air Users. Without independent termination capability, trial cannot proceed. Applicant must invest in redundancy and extensive testing (>= 50 failure scenarios) to achieve regulatory confidence. DeFOSPAM Observation-9 flagged that post-termination actions are undefined; Alexa's scale operationalizes comprehensive termination with explicit post-termination procedures.

stakeholders.name: CAA Flight Safety Investigator (REQ012)
stakeholders.specific_need: Complete recorded evidence set captured on incident (all state variables at >= 10Hz, logged for >= investigation duration, data integrity verified with checksums, forensic provenance documented) so that MOR investigation is thorough and defensible

stakeholders.name: AAIB (Air Accidents Investigation Branch)
stakeholders.specific_need: Flight data recorder equivalent: all aircraft state variables logged with synchronized timestamps, complete sensor inputs, crew actions, autonomy mode transitions, and system alerts so that post-incident forensic analysis is complete
stakeholders.target_override: Maximal logging (50Hz for high-frequency dynamics, 1-year retention) to maximize forensic information

stakeholders.name: Defence Aviation Safety Board (if MOD trial)
stakeholders.specific_need: Classified data logging and retention compliant with security protocols (JSP 440, CAP 722H) so that investigation is thorough without security compromise

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Data logging schema and retention requirements defined upfront (state variables, sampling rate, duration, storage format) so that avionics and telemetry design is scoped and cost is predictable
stakeholders.target_override: Lower sampling rate (5Hz instead of 10Hz) if event-triggered higher-rate logging is available

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: Logging and data retention plan verified in ConOps and ODD so that accident investigation capability is verified as part of safety assurance

conflicts: Sampling rate: AAIB wants 50Hz (high-frequency dynamics capture); Applicant prefers 10Hz (bandwidth/cost trade-off). Conflict: scale defaults to >= 10Hz nominal, >= 50Hz high-frequency; Applicant must justify lower rate with engineering analysis showing dynamics are captured sufficiently.

Data retention: AAIB wants long-term retention (1+ year) for systemic analysis; Applicant wants minimal retention (post-incident investigation only, then deletion) to reduce storage cost and privacy risk. Conflict: scale requires retention for full MOR investigation duration; typical AAIB investigations take 18-24 months. Applicant must provision storage accordingly.

Classified data: MOD range operator (RAISO) wants secure logging of classified test data; AAIB may need access for investigation. Conflict: security protocols (JSP 440) require sanitization of classified data before sharing with external investigators. Scale requires forensic provenance (cryptographic hash, timestamp); applicant must implement secure logging that allows investigation without breach.
reasoning: Flight Safety Investigator has regulatory authority over MOR obligations and must defend investigation completeness to AAIB. AAIB (post-incident) has power to demand maximum forensic detail. Applicant must design avionics and telemetry to capture and log all required state variables from the start. DeFOSPAM Dorothy-8 and Observation-10 flagged that data completeness and MOR trigger/action are undefined; Alexa's scale operationalizes forensic completeness with specific schema and retention requirements. MORForensicCompleteness is a top conflict hotspot (investigation completeness vs privacy vs cost).

stakeholders.name: CAA Licensing Inspector (REQ014)
stakeholders.specific_need: Documented crew competency evidence: certifications held, training completed, simulation proficiency (>= 90% emergency procedure success), and operational experience (>= min flight hours in similar conditions) so that crew licensing and authorization is defensible

stakeholders.name: CAA Human Factors Inspector (REQ010)
stakeholders.specific_need: Crew competency in swarm-specific operations (coordination, emergent behavior monitoring, termination decision-making) demonstrated via simulation and training so that meaningful human control is credible

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Specific competency domains and evidence types defined (e.g., CAA UAS operator cert, swarm-ops endorsement, 50 hours BVLOS experience) so that training plan and cost are bounded
stakeholders.tolerable_override: Waive certain certifications if equivalent simulation proficiency is demonstrated (e.g., military pilots may substitute military jet experience for UAS hours)
stakeholders.target_override: Lower min flight hours (e.g., 25 hours instead of 50) if simulation-based training is comprehensive and proficiency is high

stakeholders.name: Flight Test Crew (Pilot, Sensor Operator, Safety Officer)
stakeholders.specific_need: Training curriculum and proficiency standards clear so that crew is confident in their ability to operate the swarm safely and recover from off-nominal states

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: Crew training and competency plan documented in ConOps and Training Plan so that human error risk is addressed in safety assurance case

conflicts: Experience requirement: Licensing Inspector wants >= 50 hours BVLOS swarm experience; Applicant wants waiver if simulation proficiency is high (cost-driven, schedule-driven). Conflict: Licensing Inspector authority is absolute on waiver requests; applicant must present strong simulator validation evidence (equivalent fidelity to real flight) to negotiate. Typically, simulators provide partial credit (e.g., 25 simulator hours = 5 real flight hours).

Certification scope: Licensing Inspector wants swarm-ops-specific endorsement (formal CAA approval); Applicant may have military or commercial UAS cert but not swarm-specific. Conflict: CAA Licensing Inspector may grant conditional authorization pending swarm-ops endorsement; applicant pursues endorsement in parallel with trial.

Competency domains: Human Factors Inspector wants broad competency assessment (airmanship, decision-making, swarm monitoring, emergency procedures); Applicant wants narrow focus (system operation, basic manual recovery). Conflict: scale defines role-specific competencies (pilot: airmanship + swarm ops, sensor operator: swarm monitoring + data interpretation); applicant must address all domains in training plan.
reasoning: Licensing Inspector has regulatory authority over crew licensing and authorization and must defend crew competency to CAA and public. Human Factors Inspector shares authority on swarm-specific competency. Applicant must invest in crew training and proficiency documentation. CrewCompetencyEvidence is generally low-conflict (both regulator and applicant want qualified crew) but has scheduling implications (training time is on critical path to trial start).

stakeholders.name: CAA/MAA Liaison (REQ015)
stakeholders.specific_need: Signed apportionment matrix covers 100% of SORA hazards, 100% of design domains, and 100% of operational domains with no gaps (unassigned) and no overlaps (dual-assigned) so that regulatory responsibility is unambiguous and each regulator is accountable

stakeholders.name: CAA Case Officer (REQ001)
stakeholders.specific_need: Clear CAA regulatory scope defined in apportionment so that CAA case file is coherent and CAA responsibilities are not challenged by MAA interpretations

stakeholders.name: MAA Lead Inspector (RPAS Regulatory Branch)
stakeholders.specific_need: Clear MAA regulatory scope defined in apportionment so that MAA responsibilities are unambiguous and not in conflict with CAA expectations

stakeholders.name: Delivery Duty Holder (DDH) / Operating Duty Holder (ODH)
stakeholders.specific_need: Apportionment split reflects safety accountability: CAA responsible for public/airspace safety, MAA responsible for range/MOD safety; DDH accountable for design and operational compliance to split
stakeholders.target_override: Clear split that aligns with DDH liability and insurance scope

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Apportionment clear so that applicant knows which regulator (CAA vs MAA) to satisfy for each requirement. Avoids conflicting demands from dual regulators.

conflicts: CRITICAL CONFLICT: CAA and MAA may disagree on responsibility split: CAA may claim public-safety authority over all aspects; MAA may claim range-safety authority as primary. Example: geofence enforcement—is it CAA airspace protection (CAA) or MAA range containment (MAA)? Conflict: Alexa's scale requires both regulators to sign apportionment matrix; disagreements must be resolved before trial authorization. Liaison role (CAA/MAA Liaison) is responsible for negotiation.

Gap vs overlap: CAA/MAA may leave aspects unassigned to avoid responsibility (gap); or both may claim responsibility (overlap). Conflict: scale explicitly requires 100% coverage and zero overlaps; applicant must escalate unresolved gaps to CAA/MAA joint panel chair.

Design vs operational split: CAA may claim design authority (AMLAS, safety case); MAA may claim operational authority (range clearance, daily go/no-go). Conflict: scope of 'design domain' vs 'operational domain' must be negotiated in apportionment; typical split is CAA = aircraft design/certification, MAA = range operations/risk management.
reasoning: CAA/MAA Liaison has explicit responsibility for apportionment negotiation and must broker agreement between CAA Case Officer and MAA Lead Inspector. Without signed apportionment, trial authorization is blocked. DDH (operator) depends on clear split to structure project. Applicant has low power but high motivation (wants clarity). CAAMAAApportionmentGapFreeness is a top conflict hotspot (regulatory jurisdiction disputes); resolution requires governance escalation if Liaison cannot broker agreement.

stakeholders.name: CAA Programme Manager (REQ016)
stakeholders.specific_need: Phased trial gates with clear progression criteria (>= 4 phases), documented reversibility procedures, immutable data (write-once audit log), and re-entry procedures so that evidence builds progressively and risk is managed (gates can be reversed if new evidence suggests unacceptable risk)

stakeholders.name: CAA Safety-Case Reviewer (REQ003)
stakeholders.specific_need: Phased gates structure hazard closure: earlier gates address system architecture and component risks; later gates address integration and operational risks. Reversibility allows re-evaluation if hazard evidence changes.

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Phase progression criteria clear and achievable so that trial schedule is predictable. BUT reversibility adds uncertainty (fear that gates can be reset, delaying trial). Wants forward momentum and cost control.
stakeholders.tolerable_override: Redefine 'reversibility' as 'pause current phase and gather more data' rather than 'reset to previous phase and redo all work'
stakeholders.target_override: Fewer, larger phases (e.g., 2 phases: ground test + flight test) instead of >= 4 phases to reduce gate-crossing overhead

stakeholders.name: CAA Innovation Hub / Trial Manager
stakeholders.specific_need: Reversibility enables risk management: if phase test reveals unexpected failure mode, trial can pause, mitigation can be designed and tested, and phase can be re-entered. Supports adaptive governance.

stakeholders.name: CAA Case Officer (REQ001)
stakeholders.specific_need: Phase gates and reversibility procedures documented in case file so that trial authorization and regulatory decision points are auditable

conflicts: CRITICAL CONFLICT: Programme Manager values reversibility (risk reduction, adaptive management); Applicant wants forward momentum (cost, schedule urgency). Applicant fears that reversibility is an excuse to block trial progress indefinitely. Conflict: scale defines reversibility as documented exit criteria and re-entry procedure (not indefinite hold); applicant must know the conditions for phase reversal upfront.

Phase granularity: Programme Manager may want fine-grained phases (>= 4: ground-test, VLOS-single, VLOS-swarm, BVLOS-swarm) to manage risk; Applicant wants coarser phases (2: ground + flight) to reduce overhead. Conflict: scale requires >= 4 phases but allows flexibility in definition; typical phases are ground-system-test, VLOS-single, VLOS-swarm, BVLOS-swarm.

Data immutability: Programme Manager wants audit-locked data (write-once) so gates can be re-evaluated; Applicant prefers mutable records (easier to update if errors found). Conflict: forensic integrity requires immutable logs (CAA standard); applicant must implement write-once-read-many (WORM) data storage or cryptographic audit trail.
reasoning: Programme Manager has regulatory authority over trial schedule and gate progression and must balance risk management (reversibility) with cost/schedule efficiency. Safety-Case Reviewer depends on gates to structure hazard closure. Applicant wants schedule certainty but must accept reversibility as risk-management mechanism. DeFOSPAM Observation-11 flagged that phase gate criteria are undefined; Alexa's scale operationalizes gates with clear progression criteria, reversibility procedures, and immutable data. PhasedGateReversibility is a top conflict hotspot (risk management vs cost/schedule).

stakeholders.name: CAA Airspace Regulator (AAA) / Community Liaison
stakeholders.specific_need: CAP 1616 stakeholder engagement requirements met: >= 5 stakeholder groups engaged (residents, landowners, environmental, aviation, media), prior notification >= 30 days, documented feedback collection and response, residual concerns logged. Engagement evidence recorded in case file.

stakeholders.name: Local Communities / Non-Segregated Airspace Users
stakeholders.specific_need: Prior notification of trial operations, opportunity to voice concerns (survey, workshop, open forum), acknowledgment of feedback, and transparency on how concerns are addressed. Acceptance of trial depends on stakeholder engagement quality.
stakeholders.target_override: Maximum engagement (workshops, site visits, media briefings) to build public acceptance

stakeholders.name: Press / Media
stakeholders.specific_need: Media access to trial information, pre-flight briefings, and incident reporting so that press narrative is informed and not speculative. Opportunity to report on trial and regulate public perception.

stakeholders.name: Environmental Groups / Third Parties
stakeholders.specific_need: Engagement on environmental impacts (noise, species disturbance at dusk/night, battery fire risk) so that environmental concerns are documented and mitigations are transparent

stakeholders.name: Applicant / Operator
stakeholders.specific_need: Engagement scope and process defined upfront (stakeholder list, notification timeline, feedback mechanism, response template) so that cost and schedule are bounded. Wants minimal engagement (cost-driven).
stakeholders.tolerable_override: Lower engagement scope if trial location is remote (no residential areas within 5km); can reduce to >= 3 stakeholder groups
stakeholders.target_override: Engagement handled by CAA (applicant cost-neutral); applicant provides technical input only

stakeholders.name: CAA Case Officer (REQ001)
stakeholders.specific_need: Engagement evidence documented in case file so that trial authorization is defensible against public challenge or media scrutiny

conflicts: CRITICAL CONFLICT: Local Communities and Media want maximum engagement and transparency; Applicant wants minimal engagement (cost, schedule overhead). CAA Airspace Regulator enforces CAP 1616 minimum (>= 30 days prior notification, >= 5 stakeholder groups, documented feedback). Conflict: engagement is non-negotiable regulatory requirement; applicant cannot opt out. CAA often funds or manages engagement to ensure quality.

Environmental scope: Environmental Groups want detailed environmental assessment (noise levels, species impact, battery fire risk); Applicant wants minimal assessment (within regulatory compliance). Conflict: REQ019 (environmental impact) is absent from original requirements but implied by CAP 1616; applicant must conduct Environmental Assessment or justify exemption.

Feedback response: Communities expect substantive response to concerns (mitigation, acceptance, or documented rejection); Applicant prefers 'acknowledge and continue.' Conflict: CAP 1616 requires documented response (acceptance, mitigation, or justified rejection); applicant cannot ignore feedback.
reasoning: Airspace Regulator has authority to enforce CAP 1616 engagement requirements and must defend trial authorization to public, local communities, and media. Local Communities and Media have power (public objection, media scrutiny) to delay or block trial. Applicant must invest in engagement (cost, schedule) as prerequisite to trial approval. DeFOSPAM Flo-10 and Observation-12 flagged that REQ017 has no user story and engagement outcomes are undefined; Alexa's scale operationalizes CAP 1616 compliance with stakeholder identification, prior notification, feedback collection, and documented response. PublicEngagementCoverage is generally low-technical but high-social-risk conflict.
